concept.headline: Lambdas are anonymous functions you can store and pass around. Higher-order functions accept or return them. it names the single parameter by default.
concept.explanation: A lambda expression is { parameters -> body }. When a lambda has one parameter you can use it instead of naming it. Higher-order functions take function types as parameters (e.g. (Int) -> Int). When a lambda is the last argument to a function, it can be written outside the parentheses (trailing lambda). Kotlin's standard library — filter, map, forEach, let, apply, run — is built entirely on this mechanism.
keyPoints: Lambda: { params -> body }. Single param uses it implicitly.
Trailing lambda: last parameter can be moved outside ()
Standard library (filter, map) takes lambdas — that is why { } appears after them

demo.intro: Watch a higher-order function applyTwice run a lambda twice on an input value.

explanation.why: The lambda { it * 2 } is the f parameter. Trailing lambda syntax moves it outside the ().
explanation.intent: Show trailing lambda: last function argument outside parentheses
explanation.misconception: { it * 2 } is NOT a block of code after the function call — it IS an argument to applyTwice. The trailing lambda syntax is purely syntactic sugar.

explanation.what: f(x) = f(3) — calling the lambda with x = 3
explanation.why: applyTwice calls f first on x
explanation.intent: Show lambda being invoked — it becomes the argument value
explanation.misconception: it is the implicit name for a lambda with one parameter. Inside { it * 2 }, it holds the value passed to the lambda (3 here).

explanation.what: f(f(x)) = f(6) — applying the lambda again on the first result
explanation.why: applyTwice applies f twice — the second time on the first result
explanation.intent: Show higher-order function applying f a second time
explanation.misconception: The lambda itself does not change — the same { it * 2 } is called again, just with a different value (6 instead of 3)
applyTwice did not know about doubling — it only knew to call f twice. The behaviour was injected via the lambda.
No special syntax needed to print an Int returned from a higher-order function

explanation.what: val addTen = { n: Int -> n + 10 } — storing a lambda in a variable
explanation.why: Lambdas are values — they can be stored just like Int or String
explanation.intent: Show lambda stored as a val with explicit parameter name
explanation.misconception: When a lambda has a single param and you name it explicitly (n: Int ->), you cannot also use it. Choose one or the other.

explanation.intent: Show a named lambda passed as a function argument
explanation.misconception: Passing addTen vs { it + 10 } is equivalent — named lambdas and inline lambdas are interchangeable when the type matches

prediction.question:
What does this print?

val ops = listOf<(Int) -> Int>({ it + 1 }, { it * 3 })
println(ops[0](ops[1](2)))

explanation.afterDemo: Higher-order functions and lambdas are the foundation of Kotlin's expressive style. Every time you write .filter { } or .map { } you are passing a lambda to a higher-order function. Understanding that lambdas are values — storeable, passable, returnable — unlocks the entire standard library and makes your own APIs composable.
keyTakeaways: Lambda: { param -> body }. Single param: use it. Store as val with type (T) -> R.
Higher-order function: takes (T) -> R as a parameter — lets callers inject behaviour.
Trailing lambda: last-argument lambda moves outside () — this is why filter { } looks clean.

practice.prompt: Write a function transform(nums: List<Int>, f: (Int) -> Int): List<Int> that returns a new list with f applied to each element. Call it with listOf(1,2,3) and { it * it }, print the result.
practice.hint: Use nums.map(f) — map accepts a function reference directly.

feedback.successMessage: Correct! map(f) applies f to each element: 1*1=1, 2*2=4, 3*3=9.
retryHints: nums.map(f) or nums.map { f(it) } both work

summary.title: Lambdas & Higher-Order Functions — Complete
takeaways: Lambdas are values: { param -> body }. Single-param lambdas use it.
Higher-order functions take (T) -> R parameters — filter, map, forEach all do this.
Trailing lambda syntax places the last lambda argument outside () for readability.
